items.correct: How long the sensor is exposed to light, which affects motion blur.
items.misconception: How wide the lens opening is.
items.random_wrong: How many photos can be stored.
items.why: Students conflate shutter speed with aperture; shutter speed is a length of time, aperture is the size of the opening.

items.q: Does editing a photo digitally make it 'not real photography'?
items.correct: No — digital editing is part of photographic practice, like darkroom dodging, burning and cropping before it.
items.misconception: Yes — true photographs must be straight out of the camera.
items.random_wrong: No — editing is legally required for all photos.
items.why: Students see editing as cheating, unaware that manipulation in the darkroom long predates digital tools.

items.q: In the exposure triangle, which setting works alongside aperture and shutter speed?
items.correct: ISO — the sensor's sensitivity to light.
items.misconception: Zoom — the focal length of the lens.
items.why: Students include zoom in exposure, but focal length does not control exposure; ISO is the third variable.
Correct answer: f-stops. “ISO” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Cartier-Bresson. “Ansel Adams” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The real figure is 1842. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
